How Do Voice Chats Work?

All multiplayer Xbox Live titles must offer voice chat capability during game play. However, Xbox titles that do not support Xbox Live can also use voice chats. Because voice chats are not a feature that is specific to Xbox Live titles, you'll find the reference section for voice chats in another section of the Xbox Live documentation.

Voice is perhaps one of the more technologically demanding areas of Xbox Live support, but also perhaps one of the more rewarding. Properly handled, the Xbox Communicator, the headset and microphone device designed especially for the Xbox Live service, should easily replace the kinds of input that usually require keyboards. Voice conversation during multiplayer games is an immediate first requirement for any multiplayer game, but eventually titles may be able to provide even more radically creative features such as command-based voice recognition.

Note    Note that while multiplayer titles are required to support voice, no title can require a voice peripheral. That is, if the player has a voice peripheral, the title should detect that and support the voice chat features. However, if the player does not have a voice peripheral, the title must provide alternative access to all necessary game functionality.

As with most of the rest of the required Xbox Live features, the Xbox Live libraries already provide much of the code necessary for supporting voice chat in a title. Virtually all titles should use the Xbox high-level voice library. In rare cases, titles can call the low-level library routines provided in the Xbox Live libraries.
